summary: I was born in Alabama in 1949, and from a very young age, I had to work hard in the fields, pulling weeds from cotton and peanut patches. Life there was tough, especially for Black families like mine, and my mother told me that my father was killed before I was born when a white driver ran him over. Alabama was a place filled with prejudice and hardship, almost like slavery, but we were raised to love and survive despite it all. I didn't even get to start school because I had to work alongside everyone else in the fields.

After Alabama, I grew up in Iowa in a small town where I struggled a lot in school and was even placed in a special class at nine years old because I couldn't learn to read. My stepfather pushed me hard to learn my ABCs, but it made me resent things like vegetables and schoolwork. I dropped out in eighth grade and ran away from home when I was 14, never going back. Now at 24, I’m trying to learn to read and better myself because I want to provide for my two children and not rely on AFDC.

My family was Latin, and both my mother and father were from Puerto Rico, but we lived in New York City. Growing up, no one really had time to read to me because they were busy trying to learn English and survive in a tough environment. I never had the chance to be read to by my father, and that made reading feel like an impossible dream for me. Despite knowing I had a good mind and wanting to learn, there was no one who truly cared enough to help me.
cues: mother and father from Puerto Rico

summary: I lived in New York City with my Puerto Rican family, and it was a hard place to grow up. My father was often violent, and I was involved with gangs as early as five years old because I was scared and felt safer outside than at home. Life in New York was full of fear and struggle, and no one had time to nurture me or help me with reading, which made me feel isolated and behind. Despite all this, I’m still trying to push forward and improve my life.
